items.name: The Keystone
items.meaning: The wedge capstone that locks the arch — the single piece that locks your future in place at the top of the climb.
items.characterization: A trapezoidal capstone with an upward-chevron aperture cut into its base; the soul is one warm light that breathes up through that passage — the glow from the floor above, the reward of getting in and ascending.
items.safety: Reads as an architectural capstone with an ascending doorway; the pointed (not rounded) aperture and flat trapezoid top keep it clear of any padlock/keyhole or generic delta/triangle misread, and it holds as one nameable shape at 24px grayscale.

items.name: The Keystone Gate
items.meaning: A keystone — the cap-stone you earn — whose negative space opens as a lit gateway: the threshold you cross by getting IN.
items.characterization: Its soul is the warm light at the base of the doorway, breathing slowly as if the building is lit and waiting for you to step through — a calm beacon, not a mascot.
items.safety: Grounded keystone wedge with one arched opening reads cleanly as a gate/threshold; no taper-to-tip, orb-on-stalk, or paired-rounds — no unintended object or anatomy at any size or rotation.

items.name: The Ascent
items.meaning: An elevator shaft rising to a chamfered apex — the climb up the floors to the offer, with the way in cut into the negative-space channel itself.
items.characterization: The whole mark is a still gold monolith; its soul is the single cream light-capsule that rises and breathes inside the shaft — one calm pulse of "someone is moving up in here" that can travel into the elevator UI and loading states without ever changing the silhouette.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as an architectural shaft/pylon with a lit aperture; the flat chamfered crown and tall parallel channel keep it from collapsing into an upward arrow, a SIM-card/tag, a keyhole, a letter, or any anatomy at 24px grayscale.

items.name: Keysmith
items.meaning: A keyhole carved into a single gold tower-mass: the round crown is the building's summit, the descending shaft is the elevator — the hole IS the way in, and the way up to an offer.
items.characterization: It carries identity as a building lit from within: at rest a quiet sealed emblem, alive a warm light breathes up the shaft and blooms in the crown — the Tower noticing you've arrived and unlocking.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as a keyhole-in-a-monolith at 24px grayscale; the flared base and rounded crown keep it from misreading as a literal brass key, a person, a vase, or an exclamation mark — no unintended anatomy or object.

items.name: The Monogram
items.meaning: A capital T built as a tower — the stem is the shaft, the crossbar is the penthouse cap — with the climb hidden inside as a lit elevator car rising in the negative-space channel.
items.characterization: Its soul is the single cream window breathing in the dark shaft: an elevator car arriving, the building quietly alive and waiting for you to step in.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as the letter T / a tower with a lit interior; the contained window and shaft avoid any keyhole, exclamation-mark, or anatomical misread, and the silhouette holds as one nameable shape at 24px grayscale.

items.name: The Lit Window
items.meaning: A single tall tower-window left glowing gold against a dark vertical mass — the light kept on for you; the moment of getting seen, getting in.
items.characterization: The soul is one breathing aperture: a luminous arched portal with a doorway-threshold cut into its base, so identity radiates from a single warm light you step through rather than from any drawn building.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as a lit window/threshold portal at 24px grayscale; the centered arched doorway-notch keeps it from misreading as a keyhole, robe-figure, tombstone, or literal building.

items.name: The Seal
items.meaning: A wax-seal medallion ring crowning a keystone — the offer institutionally sealed; the arched doorway cut into the keystone's base is the "getting IN" rendered in negative space.
items.characterization: It carries the brand as a crest: a calm authority on every floor plate and elevator panel, with its soul a warm light that breathes from inside the open doorway — the building quietly welcoming you in.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as "a crested ring with a doorway/keystone"; the flat-based, flat-topped keystone wedge and enclosing ring avoid any tooth, keyhole, or anatomical misread, and it holds as a single nameable shape down to 16px grayscale.

items.name: The Threshold
items.meaning: An Art-Deco arched portal — the gateway you cross to step into opportunity; the way in lives in the wide negative space beneath the arch.
items.characterization: It carries the brand as a doorway that is always lit for you: the still gold arch is the building's permanence and poise, while the single ember breathing on the threshold is the soul — a warm "you may enter" that pulses like a heartbeat at the base of every screen.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as an arched gateway with a light on the floor; the wide counter and low-seated dot prevent any keyhole/key, padlock, lamp, or anatomy misread, and it holds as one nameable shape down to 16px grayscale.

items.name: The Beacon
items.meaning: An Art-Deco setback spire crowned by a lit beacon — guidance and ambition: the tower you enter is the one that lights your way up.
items.characterization: It carries identity through one breathing element — the beacon at the apex pulses like a heartbeat, so a still gold mark feels quietly alive and watchful, the building's soul kept lit.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as a stepped tower with a lit tip and an arched entrance; the halo + cross-ray burst register as light rather than a head, so there is no orb-on-stalk, face, or figure misread, and the doorway encodes "getting in" without looking like a keyhole or arrow.

items.name: The Climb
items.meaning: Stacked floor-strata of a tower with one floating, lit destination band — your ascent through the pipeline toward the top floor.
items.characterization: A mid-abstract Bloomberg-data tower: equal-width floor-bars grounded on a slab with a negative-space doorway ("getting IN"), crowned by one band that has visibly risen above the stack — its soft breathing halo is the single living light, calm and architectural rather than warm/sparky.
items.safety: Reads as a stacked tower of floors with a doorway notch and an elevated lit floor; equal-width bars avoid any pyramid/Christmas-tree or hamburger-menu misread, the centered base cutout reads as an entrance (not legs/feet), and no spark, key, ladder, arrow, or anatomy is implied.

items.name: The Arrival
items.meaning: Two gold elevator doors parting on a vertical sliver of light — the navigation metaphor itself: the reveal of arriving on your floor.
items.characterization: Its soul is the breathing light in the gap — the threshold glow swells and settles like a held breath, so the doors feel forever caught in the instant of opening for you; 'getting IN' lives entirely in the negative space.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as a parting portal/threshold of light, not a pause icon, a book, or any figure — the arched outer shoulders and the apex-overshoot light spike keep it architectural and unambiguous at 24px grayscale.

items.name: The Pillar
items.meaning: A single load-bearing column with light rising inside it — grounded stability that ascends, the cool architectural inverse of the warm AI spark.
items.characterization: Its soul is the slim inner light-shaft that breathes upward like a held breath: the column stays constant and rooted while the glow within is always quietly rising — a structure that is alive.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as one column/keystone primitive at 24px grayscale; the inner notch is centered and symmetric so it never resolves into a letterform, slot, anatomy, or a literal building portrait.

items.name: The Apex
items.meaning: The purest ascent gesture — a peaked chevron resolving to a single point, the moment of arrival at the top of the tower.
items.characterization: Its soul is the rooftop lit from within: a soft warm light rises up the mark and the apex quietly breathes, so a flat geometric peak feels like a building awake at the summit.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as an abstract peaked chevron with a notched threshold base; the symmetric grid and overshoot keep it from misreading as a literal letter "A", arrowhead, mountain, or any anatomy.

items.name: The Crown
items.meaning: A three-step Art-Deco setback cap — the top floor, the win — with the way IN carved as a lit arched portal in the negative space.
items.characterization: Soul lives in the single breathing light that rises to fill the arched doorway — a still, premium "threshold glow" that makes the crown feel inhabited and earned rather than regal-kitsch; it carries across the cast as a recurring lit-portal motif.
items.safety: Verified at 120/48/24px and 24px grayscale: reads as a stepped crown/tower-cap with a doorway; symmetric wide tiers + arch deliberately avoid the stalk/candle/keyhole/anatomy misreads seen in earlier asymmetric drafts.

items.name: The Summit
items.meaning: An abstract peak fused with a spire — the ascent toward the offer, with the way IN carved as a portal at its base.
items.characterization: Light lives at the apex and breathes there — the destination glows from the top of the climb, giving an otherwise still emblem a quiet, summoning soul.
items.safety: Reads as a single peak with a doorway/threshold at its base; no apex dot, so no exclamation-mark, lowercase-i, robed-figure, or beacon/antenna misread, and the outline never shifts under the glow.

items.name: The Lodestar
items.meaning: A guiding star crowning a slender spire — the fixed point you navigate your whole search by.
items.characterization: Its soul is the single breathing glint at the apex: a calm, patient beacon that never blinks out, the building's eye watching over the climb.
items.safety: Reads cleanly as star-over-spire; the four-point glint risks rhyming with the generic AI-spark/asterisk and could read as a literal star — exactly the monoculture trap research flagged.

flags: window-light candidate shipped as SVG:placeholder — I synthesized a faithful 'dark tower mass + one lit tall window' to render and judge it; scores reflect that reconstruction, not author-supplied art.

SILHOUETTE MISREADS confirmed in pixels: archway reads as HEADPHONES / horseshoe magnet; floor-stack reads as a HAMBURGER MENU icon (fatal for a brand mark); pillar-light reads as a COFFEE/TO-GO CUP or trophy; keyhole-tower reads as LOCK/SECURITY/password, off-metaphor; seal-keystone reads as a generic coin/medallion/button blob; keystone-threshold reads as a DRINKING GLASS/tumbler.
AI-TROPE / CLICHE flags the brief explicitly warns against: chevron-apex is the generic UP-ARROW/ascent caret; lodestar is the STAR cliche; both are well-executed but borrow a tired primitive.

The two literal-skyscraper candidates (spire-beacon, summit) trade ownability for instant readability — spire-beacon is the most legibly 'tower' but flirts with stock Art-Deco building clip-art; summit reads as a TENT/mountain at small size.

Soul test (one breathing light element): rendered animated variants for t-tower (glowing elevator window), spire-beacon (pulsing beacon), ascent-shaft (elevator car rising in shaft), keystone (glowing gate). All four sustain a single living-light focal point; t-tower's window-in-letterform is the most ownable home for it.

ranking.verdict: WINNER (my lens). The only mark that is simultaneously ownable AND bulletproof at every scale: at native 24px the T is crisp and unmistakable while the lit elevator window detail gracefully drops out; the silhouette is a clean, confident letterform. It crystallizes the building metaphor (stem=shaft, crossbar=penthouse cap) without being literal clip-art, owns architectural whitespace, and gives the 'one breathing light' a perfect home in the negative-space window. Monogram = inherently brandable, trademarkable, and timeless (Bloomberg/Apple register). Risk: a bare slab-serif T is generic UNTIL the tower proportions + lit window do the work — so the styling must commit to the window as the soul, or it collapses to a font glyph. Highest premium-per-pixel of the set.

Strong runner-up. The most instantly READABLE 'tower' in the pixels — the stepped Art-Deco setback spire holds its skyscraper read even at 24px, and the pulsing beacon is a natural single-light soul. Best literal embodiment of 'a skyscraper you enter.' But it pays for legibility with ownability: it edges toward generic Empire-State/Chrysler stock-building territory, and the beacon glint vanishes at 24px leaving a slightly busy mass. Premium and on-brief, just less distinctively yours than a monogram.

Conceptually the richest meaning (the capstone that locks your future) and the most genuinely mid-abstract/ownable primitive — NOT a borrowed icon. Hero is elegant. But the pixels expose the flaw: at 24px the inner gate negative space collapses and the mark reads as a featureless wedge / tooth / bell; silhouette is ambiguous. The 'getting IN' gate needs more aperture to survive small. Highest upside if the negative-space gate is enlarged; as drawn it under-reads.

Beautiful concept and the animated elevator-car-rising test is the most literally on-metaphor motion in the set. But the static pixels misfire: at 24px the chamfered capsule reads as a PADLOCK / battery / door-handle, and the window detail disappears. The shaft-as-negative-space is too subtle to carry the idea without motion. Premium surface, weak standalone silhouette.

Adds a lit gateway to the keystone — good intent on 'getting IN via negative space.' But the decorative ring is noise, and in pixels the mass reads as a DRINKING GLASS/tumbler at hero and a generic lozenge at 24px. The keystone idea is better served by the cleaner 'keystone' candidate; this version dilutes it.

On-metaphor (the nav gesture itself) and the cream light-sliver is a nice soul element, but the sliver is too thin to survive: at 24px it reads as a solid square / closed book, and the silhouette loses the 'parting' read entirely. Needs the gap to be bolder. Decent but not distinctive.

Cleanest silhouette in the set and survives 24px flawlessly — but it is exactly the up-arrow/ascent CLICHE the brief tells us to avoid, reads as a tent or an Adobe-style A, and carries zero of the building metaphor. Technically crisp, conceptually borrowed. Disqualified by lens.

The stepped setback cap is a nice 'top floor / the win' idea and the deco is premium at hero, but at 24px the silhouette muddles into a faucet/sink/bridge shape with the arched portal lost. The crown read isn't reliable. Middling.

Abstract peak-fused-spire with a base portal — ascent metaphor is clear and the apex glow is a real soul element, but the pixels read as a TENT / mountain at small size, colliding with outdoor/adventure brands. Off the 'architecture' positioning. Capable craft, wrong neighborhood.

Elegant and premium at hero (star-on-spire = guidance), but it is the STAR cliche the brief warns against, it carries no building metaphor, and at 24px the star points collapse to a dot-on-a-stick. Pretty, not ownable, off-brief.

Most legible at 24px of the abstract group (keyhole holds clearly) and clever 'the hole IS the way in,' but a keyhole is a borrowed/literal icon that screams LOCK / SECURITY / password manager, not luxury tower. Strong silhouette serving the wrong meaning.

Ambitious (ring + keystone + arched door) but too many ideas at once — in pixels it reads as a generic coin/medallion/button with a blob, and at 24px it's an indistinct circle. Busy, not ownable, low premium-per-pixel.

Fatal pixel misread: at 24px it is indistinguishable from a HAMBURGER MENU / list-lines UI icon, and the silhouette confirms it. A brand symbol cannot read as a system control. The floating top band soul is nice but cannot save it. Eliminate.

Threshold idea is on-brief but the pixels are damning: at every size it reads as HEADPHONES or a horseshoe magnet, not a doorway. The portal-as-negative-space inverts into the wrong object. Eliminate.

Single load-bearing column with inner light is a clean 'cool architectural inverse of the AI spark' concept, but in pixels the capital+shaft reads as a COFFEE/TO-GO CUP or trophy at hero and small. The object misread kills the premium architectural intent. Eliminate.

(Author SVG was placeholder; judged on my faithful reconstruction.) The 'one lit window kept on for you' is the purest expression of the soul brief, but dark-on-dark mass means the tower body disappears and at 24px it is a featureless dark rectangle with a faint cross — almost nothing reads. The single-light idea is gold but needs a different carrier (e.g. the t-tower window). As a standalone mark it under-reads badly.

flags: keystone-threshold: SILHOUETTE MISREAD — reads unmistakably as a disposable coffee/drink cup (wide-top, narrow-base mass inside a ring); at 24px collapses to a bright blob. Disqualifying object misread.
archway: SILHOUETTE MISREAD — bare white form reads as a horseshoe-magnet / over-ear headphones; the dot adds clutter rather than soul at small sizes.
pillar-light: OBJECT MISREAD — capped column reads as a USB plug / AA battery / trash bin; the light slot becomes a meaningless black notch under ~40px.
seal-keystone: COIN/BLOB COLLAPSE — at 24px the ring + inner arch mush into a coin with a dot; the keystone wedge is invisible at hero already.
ascent-shaft: MISREAD — horizontal pill window reads as a luggage tag / keycard / phone button; the horizontal accent fights the vertical ascent it's supposed to encode.
lodestar: FAILS REDUCTION — slender spire is too thin; at 24/16px it vanishes to a faint vertical line + dot (sparkler/pin), not a tower.
spire-beacon: FAILS REDUCTION — beautiful at hero but over-detailed; smudges to an indistinct vertical blob at 24px, apex glint gone.
elevator-doors: SOUL LOST + MISREAD — collapses to a solid rectangle reading as a closed book/folder; the light sliver (its entire concept) is invisible below 40px.
floor-stack: GENERIC MISREAD — stacked bars read as a hamburger-menu / list icon; no tower legible at any size.
t-tower: SOUL LOST — the lit elevator window (the one breathing light element) disappears below ~40px, leaving a literal letter T.
window-light: NO SUBMISSION — placeholder only, not judgeable.
keystone: notch fills in at 24/16px → reads as a plain solid trapezoid/lozenge, losing the keystone read.

RENDER METHOD: each SVG written to HTML, rendered via headless Chrome --screenshot at hero(180px), 24px, and bare white-on-black silhouette (gradients/cream forced white, navy holes forced black); plus a 64/40/24/16px downscale ramp on cream for the finalists.

ranking.verdict: WINNER from the legibility/silhouette lens. The ONLY candidate that survives flawlessly at every scale tested — clean, instantly-read keyhole at hero, 24px, AND 16px, with a perfectly ownable silhouette (round crown = summit, flared shaft = elevator). Negative space IS the 'way in' and it never collapses to a coin or blob. Premium, mid-abstract, architectural. The crown carries a natural lit-window/breathing-light slot for the soul element. No unintended object misread — 'keyhole' is exactly the intended read. Best reducer in the set by a clear margin.

Strongest pure reducer alongside the keyhole — bold A-frame/caret that holds crisply from hero to 16px with zero collapse and a clean silhouette. BUT it is a generic 'up/arrow/play' gesture (Vimeo-ish caret), the opposite of ownable, and offers no negative-space 'getting IN' and no obvious home for the breathing light. Silhouette-safe but not crystallizing the building metaphor.

Surprisingly robust reducer — the stepped Art-Deco silhouette stays distinctive and jagged-ownable even at 16px, and the negative-space arched portal survives as a clean cut-out (genuine 'way in'). Reads as deco-tower-crown, not a cliche. Slightly busy and edges toward castle-merlon, but it carries character and a clear lit-arch slot for soul. A real contender on silhouette grounds.

On-metaphor and clean at hero, but the legibility test exposes a flaw: at 24px and 16px the triangular notch fills in and it flattens to a solid trapezoid/lozenge (faintly bucket/lampshade), losing the 'keystone' idea and the negative-space gate. Silhouette is simple and safe but bland once reduced; no surviving light element. Decent, not distinctive under reduction.

Excellent raw legibility — a clean T at every size — but it reduces to a literal LETTER T (a monogram, not a mid-abstract primitive), and its soul (the lit elevator window) is gone below ~40px, leaving nothing breathing. Silhouette-safe but on the wrong axis: too literal/generic to own the architectural whitespace.

Holds as a clean filled peak at small sizes, but reads as a MOUNTAIN/tent, not a tower — wrong primitive (and crowds the warm 'summit/peak' space rather than the cool architectural one). The negative-portal at the base is too subtle to survive reduction. Safe silhouette, off-brief read.

Gorgeous, premium, characterful deco spire at HERO — but it fails the core lens: over-detailed, it smudges to an indistinct vertical blob at 24px and the apex beacon (its soul) vanishes. Does not survive reduction; silhouette too intricate.

Tall rounded mass with a HORIZONTAL pill window — the horizontal accent fights the vertical ascent, and the silhouette/24px reads as a luggage tag, keycard, or phone with a button. Object misread plus a soul element pointed the wrong way. Reduces to a generic pill.

Bare silhouette reads as a HORSESHOE-MAGNET / headphones; the lit dot reads as clutter, not soul, and survives reduction only as 'inverted-U + dot.' Clear unintended object misread that gets worse, not better, when stripped to silhouette.

Elegant at hero but FAILS reduction hard — the slender spire is too thin to hold; at 24/16px it dissolves to a faint vertical line with a dot (sparkler/pin/wand), not a tower. Worst reducer of the near-misses; silhouette has almost no mass to read.
n/a

Classic COIN/BLOB COLLAPSE — at 24px the ring + tiny inner arch mush into a coin with an indistinct dot, and even at hero the keystone wedge is barely legible inside the medallion. Too much nested detail in a small frame; silhouette reads as a generic seal, not the building.

Capped column reads as a USB plug / battery / trash bin in silhouette; the central light slot becomes a meaningless black notch and disappears under ~40px (soul lost). Reduces to a generic capsule. Object misread.

Collapses to a solid rectangle reading as a closed BOOK/folder; the vertical light sliver — the entire concept and its only breathing element — is invisible below ~40px. Silhouette safe-as-a-block but says nothing; metaphor evaporates on reduction.

Stacked bars read as a HAMBURGER-MENU / list icon at every size — generic UI furniture, no tower legible, no ownable silhouette. The floating gold band reads as just another menu line when shrunk. Off-brief and unownable.
NO SUBMISSION — SVG is a literal 'placeholder' string; nothing to render or judge. Cannot be scored on actual pixels.

My top pick for character + getting-IN. A single gold tower-mass carved into a keyhole: the round crown is a summit, the descending shaft is the elevator, and the hole literally IS the way in. Unmistakable at every scale — holds perfectly at 24px and in bare silhouette with zero ambiguity. Most characterizable in the set: the round crown reads as an eye/head and the body as a calm standing posture, so an idle-blink or breathing-glow soul element lands naturally. Encodes 'getting IN' as pure negative space without a literal door cliche. Only knock: keyhole is a known security-app trope, so it's slightly less architectural than keystone — but the tower-mass framing rescues it into THIS app.

The cleanest pure idea and the truest to the building metaphor: a wedge capstone with an arched doorway cut into its negative space — the piece that locks your future at the top, with 'getting IN' as the portal. Premium, architectural, owns the cool whitespace the research demands. Reads at 24px and in silhouette holds as a distinct trapezoid+arch. Characterizable: the arch is a niche where a glow/figure can live. Risk I flagged in pixels: at the very smallest size the inner arch flattens toward a generic up-triangle/play-button, so the lit element must carry the soul. Strong front-runner.

Stepped Art-Deco setback crown with a lit arched portal punched dead center — genuinely says 'tower top + the win + the way in' at once. The strongest fusion of summit and threshold in the set. Survives silhouette (stepped pyramid + arch both read) and the central arch doubles as a face-like negative space, so it can be characterized with posture/calm. Soul lives in the lit portal. Minor flag: leans toward a ziggurat/Aztec-temple read at hero and is a touch busy at 24px, but the architecture intent stays legible.

Clean Deco arched portal with a gold dot at the threshold — strong 'getting IN' and a built-in single light/soul element (the dot reads as a person or a kept-on lamp). Holds in silhouette. Flags: the two side-piers + central arch + dot can misread as a bell or lamp, and the bare arch is a common doorway cliche the brief warns near. Characterizable via the dot, but the form itself has limited posture. Solid mid-tier.

Ring + keystone-with-arch reads institutional and premium — the offer 'sealed.' Holds at 24px and silhouette. But it's three ideas stacked (ring + keystone + portal), so ownability suffers and it drifts toward generic-medallion. The inner keystone is small, limiting characterizability. Respectable but not the single ownable primitive the research asks for.

Reads as a tent/mountain-mass with a doorway slit cut into the base — the negative-space door is legible and the silhouette holds. But the apex glow is invisible in pixels, and the form skews outdoors/camping rather than skyscraper, so rootedness in THIS building metaphor is weak. Decent but semantically adjacent to the wrong world.

Intended a keystone whose negative space opens as a lit gateway, but the pixels betray it: the bottom radial 'floor' glow makes the form read as a shopping bag or a paint/cosmetic tube inside a ring. The enclosing circle adds premium but muddies the silhouette into a bag shape. Object misread is the killer here — the gateway intent does not survive rendering.

Crisp, premium, reads as a letter-A / tent / chevron at every scale with excellent craft and ownable geometry. But it IS the ascent-arrow/chevron cliche the brief explicitly warns against, carries zero building metaphor, has no negative-space 'getting IN,' and no soul element. Pure gesture, no character — fails my rootedness+characterizability lens despite being the most technically clean shape.

Elegant and premium at hero with a nice single glint as the soul element, but the pixels read unmistakably as an awards trophy / Oscar statuette (tapered body + base + star), which is semantically off for an internship climb and brushes the 'star' cliche the research flags. Silhouette holds but as a trophy, not a tower. Moderate ownability, limited building rootedness.

Stacked floor-strata with a doorway notch in the bottom band. The pixels read as plain stacked bars / a list / hamburger-menu rows — the 'lit destination band' just looks like the top stripe and has no special read. At 24px it's indistinguishable from a generic list icon. No single soul element, no posture, low ownability. The door notch is buried.

Intended an elevator shaft to a chamfered apex with a lit pill-window, but the rendered form reads as a luggage/gift tag (or keycard) with a button. The lit pill is a nice soul candidate, yet the outer tag shape is ambiguous and not tower-like. Object misread undercuts rootedness despite clean small-scale legibility.

Two gold panels parting on a light sliver — but the rounded-top panels make the pixels read as an open book / passport / folder, not elevator doors. The central light slit is a genuine breathing-soul element, the strongest thing here, but the doors metaphor does not survive the shape. Silhouette = book. Object misread.

At hero the pixels read unmistakably as a church/cathedral — arched base + spire + cross-like glint = a chapel, which is an off-brief semantic misread for an internship command center. Detail-heavy, the beacon glint is lost at 24px, and the religious read is a serious flag. Premium-ish craft but wrong object.

Intended a T-as-tower with a lit elevator window, but the pixels read as a flashlight or a hammer/mallet with a button — the crossbar is too short/heavy to register as a clean letterform and the lit window all but disappears. A literal initial isn't the building primitive the brief wants anyway. Object misread + weak metaphor.

Intended a load-bearing column with rising inner light, but the rendered shape reads definitively as a takeaway coffee cup (cap + tapered body + the dark vertical seam). Total object misread that inverts the premium intent. Clean at small sizes, but as a cup, not a pillar. Fails rootedness outright.

No SVG was provided (placeholder), so nothing was rendered or judged. Auto-fails on craft/availability; the concept (a single lit tower-window as the soul/getting-seen moment) is promising but cannot be scored on pixels.

flags: window-light: no SVG supplied (literal 'placeholder') — not rendered, auto-failed.
pillar-light: silhouette/object MISREAD — reads as a takeaway coffee cup, not a column.
t-tower: object MISREAD — reads as a flashlight/mallet; the letter T and lit window do not register.
elevator-doors: object MISREAD — rounded panels read as an open book/passport, not elevator doors.
spire-beacon: object MISREAD — reads as a church/cathedral (cross-like glint), off-brief.
keystone-threshold: object MISREAD — bottom glow makes it read as a shopping bag / cosmetic tube.
ascent-shaft: object MISREAD — reads as a luggage/gift tag with a button, not an elevator shaft.
lodestar: reads as an awards trophy/Oscar statuette; also brushes the 'star' cliche the brief warns against.
floor-stack: at 24px collapses into a generic list/hamburger-menu icon; lit band does not read.
chevron-apex: IS the ascent-chevron/arrow cliche the brief explicitly warns against; no building metaphor, no soul element.
All silhouettes generated via CSS grayscale+contrast threshold (approximate flat mask), not a true alpha cutout.

decision.rationale: Keyhole-tower is the autonomous pick. Under the brief's mandate to weight silhouette-safety and rootedness heavily, it is the only candidate that BOTH wins lenses (legibility AND meaning each crowned it independently) AND tops the field on the math: highest simple cross-lens average (7.83) and highest silhouette/rootedness-weighted average (7.96), nearly a full point clear of second. It owns the dimension that decides this round — silhouette-safety averages 9.3 (two perfect 10s) because the negative-space 'way in' IS the intended read and never collapses into a coin, blob, or hamburger icon at 24px or 16px; both reducing lenses confirmed it survives to 16px, the best reducer in the set. It satisfies every hard line of the brief: a single gold tower-mass (round crown = summit, flared shaft = elevator), 'getting IN' encoded purely in negative space with no door cliche, reads at 24px grayscale, and the crown carries a natural lit-window slot for the one breathing light. It is also the most characterizable (crown-as-head/eye supports an idle-blink or breathing-glow), which feeds the character-driven Tower world. The single knock — keyhole is a security-app trope — is real but is the LEAST severe risk in the field (a styling/framing problem the tower-mass already rescues), versus the structural failures that disqualify the alternatives: t-tower (brand-lens winner) loses its entire soul below ~40px and misreads as a flashlight/mallet, failing the brief's own '24px + breathing light' test; chevron-apex and lodestar are the exact up-arrow/star cliches the brief forbids; every literal-skyscraper and threshold candidate misreads as a tangible object (coffee cup, headphones, book, coin, tent, luggage tag). Keyhole-tower is the one mark that is simultaneously bulletproof at scale, ownable as a mid-abstract architectural primitive, on-metaphor, and home to the soul light.

perItem.verdict: WINNER — only mark that wins lenses AND tops weighted math; silhouette 9.3, negative-space 'way in' never collapses, natural soul-light slot, most characterizable.
Alt 1 — highest rootedness in field (7.7), truest architectural-whitespace primitive; aperture must be enlarged or the gate flattens to a play-button at small sizes.
Alt 2 — dark horse; stepped deco silhouette and lit arched portal survived reduction robustly across all three lenses; slight ziggurat/busy risk at 24px.
Alt 3 — won brand lens on a crisp T, but soul-light vanishes below ~40px and reads as flashlight/mallet; cannot win on the brief's 24px-breathing-light test.
Crispest silhouette but IS the up-arrow/ascent cliche the brief forbids; zero building metaphor, no negative-space entry, no soul. Disqualified by brief.
Most legibly 'tower' at hero but fails reduction — smudges to a vertical blob at 24px, beacon gone; meaning lens read it as a church. Stock-deco ownability risk.
Clear ascent read but reads as tent/mountain at small size — wrong (outdoor) neighborhood; apex glow invisible in pixels.
Best on-metaphor motion (rising car) but static misreads as padlock/luggage-tag/keycard; horizontal window fights the vertical ascent.
Three ideas stacked (ring+keystone+arch); collapses to a generic coin/medallion at 24px. Low premium-per-pixel.
Disqualifying object misread — reads as a drinking glass/tumbler/shopping bag; the cleaner 'keystone' serves this idea better.
Threshold intent on-brief but silhouette inverts to headphones / horseshoe-magnet at every size. Eliminate.
On-metaphor nav gesture but rounded panels read as a closed book/folder; the light sliver — its whole concept — is invisible below ~40px.
Star cliche the brief warns against; reads as an Oscar/trophy, no building metaphor, dissolves to dot-on-a-stick at 24px.
Fatal — indistinguishable from a hamburger-menu/list icon at every size. A brand mark cannot read as a system control. Eliminate.
Clean 'architectural inverse' concept but reads definitively as a takeaway coffee cup; the object misread inverts the premium intent. Eliminate.
No submission (SVG placeholder); purest soul concept but unjudgeable and, on reconstruction, under-reads to a dark rectangle. Needs a different carrier (the keyhole crown).
